text: vicious circle: absolute power was erected as a barrier to protect against self-asserting individuals, but as such it also kept producing them. From the analysis offered, we can start to pin down what might be so problematic about seventeenth century modernity – our modernity. In the search for Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 137 a ‘first principle’, both Hobbes and Descartes found their answer in a real liminal experience: universal fear and endless doubt. The particularity of their construct, however, was that instead of replacing that fear or doubt with the establishment of society on the basis of a new normality, they turned these liminal experiences par excellence into the new building blocks of the new reality. As Voegelin said it, the search for the ground thus remains recognizable as the reality experienced even in the modes of deformation. Hobbes and Descartes are liminal thinkers in this very Weberian sense: they departed from an ‘image of the world’, and then ‘took a stand in the face of the world’ by rationalizing the image and devising a system from it. They recognized and entered liminality by declaring the world radically and finally alien to the self. At the same time, from that experience of liminality, from that experience of fear and diabolic doubt, standing against the world, they proposed a fundament and a way out. Hobbes and Descartes took us through liminality and ended it by establishing a new order; but they did so by establishing liminality at its core. Such a foundation will always have a problem with its own limits: it is inherently prone to that hubris and that excess that the ancient Greeks had recognized as a real danger, and which the Platonic soul was meant to counter. While Hobbes’ and Descartes’ continued attacks on the ancients and Aristotelianism were very explicit attempts to overcome mediaeval thinking, one could equally well claim that what Hobbes and Descartes jointly succeeded in doing was to complete the undoing of the (Italian) Renaissance ideals. They did so by definitively tearing apart the two threads that Renaissance thinking had carefully tried to recover and unite: the life and teaching of Christ (with a stress on Jesus as an exemplary prophet) and Greek philosophy (with a stress on the unity between truth, beauty and order). In Renaissance philosophy, those were the threads that could sustain a new-found subjectivity (Szakolczai 2007). In this vein, the Enlightenment period that developed a century later simply confirmed that separation, although with a different stress. While seventeenth century philosophy filtered away the Hellenic inspiration, the nineteenth century Enlightenment did the same with Christianity, despiritualizing Hellenic thought exactly as Hobbes and Descartes had despiritualized Christianity. The standard reference to the Renaissance as a forerunner to the Enlightenment is on that account simply not tenable. The secularization that followed from the Cartesian and Hobbesian systems of thought is paradoxical, for neither Hobbes nor Descartes intended the rational systems they devised to refute divinity; quite the contrary. One can indeed argue that both Descartes and Hobbes wanted to safeguard Christianity, and in a very similar way: by making it congruent with a new scientific theory; indeed, by making God’s existence (Descartes) and the Holy Scriptures (Hobbes) the foundation of a new scientific worldview. Descartes had no desire to rule out the transcendental realm. Proving God’s existence was a crucial matter to him. But he claimed God existed insofar – and only insofar – as it was revealed through the truth of the self as mind. The knowing mind is therefore not Godless (as Descartes would have to Liminality and the Modern 138 reassure his troubled Christian public). It stands in the closest proximity to God himself. It stands indeed so close that any contradiction between God and Pure Knowledge becomes meaningless. The conditions of pure reason and truth of the Cartesian self are God himself. They are the same. As it turns out, this is not a separation of divinity from the sphere of knowledge, leaving the religious sphere autonomous but without influence in the this-worldly realm. Rather, it is a total control of divinity – and in that sense a total destruction. What is carried out here is not a separation of politics from a religious context. It is rather ‘the elimination of the life of the spirit from public representation and the corresponding contraction of politics to a secular nucleus’ (Voegelin 1998: 23, italics added). As Hobbes did from the perspective of the state, so Descartes accomplished from the perspective of the self. Knowledge should be reached through pure reason whose indisputable truth resided within itself, which it itself represented, and that could not be brought to judgment by any external source of power but itself. The Hobbesian state and the Cartesian self are on this note strikingly similar in attributes, and suggest a correspondence between our concepts of state and self which is much more than incidental. Despite its secularizing effects on thought and governance, this commonality can best be grasped by staying within its original religious discourse: the common ground for the absolute self and the absolute state was the final reversal of the structure of revelation. From this analysis, a different understanding of ‘absolutism’ becomes possible. Reversing the structure of revelation did not imply that the transcendental realm ceased to exist but rather that it would take on new forms. Kant’s transcendental subject is transcendental because it pulls itself up by its own bootstraps, not because it finds its necessary condition of being in a divinely ordained order of nature: it is transcendence through detachment, and from that detachment order is bestowed on nature, and from that quality of order-bestowing its divinity is established. Man is divine through his ordering capacities. Similarly, the structure of the apparently a-religious political revolutions that swept Europe from the nineteenth century carried on the structure of transcendence just as well, only that the transcendent was now brought down to install a this-worldly future-orientation: a ‘horizontal transcendence’, to use another
